title: The Division of Labor in Society
originalTitle: De la division du travail social
note: 1997 English edition, Free Press / University of Chicago Press, translated by W.D. Halls with introduction by Lewis A. Coser. Page count verified per publisher description (University of Chicago Press 1997 edition).
excerpt: The foundational work of modern sociology in which Durkheim argues that the division of labor is the primary source of social solidarity, distinguishing mechanical solidarity in traditional societies from organic solidarity in modern ones, and warning that unchecked economic individualism produces anomie — a condition of normlessness threatening social order.
keyIdeas: Social solidarity is not a sentiment but a social fact — a binding moral force with objective existence
Mechanical solidarity binds traditional societies through shared beliefs and moral likeness; organic solidarity binds modern societies through functional interdependence
The division of labor is the primary mechanism transforming societies from mechanical to organic solidarity
Anomie — the breakdown of normative regulation — arises when economic relations outpace moral regulation, producing social disorder
Crime is a normal and necessary feature of all societies because it affirms and clarifies collective boundaries
Law types mirror solidarity types: repressive law corresponds to mechanical solidarity; restitutive law corresponds to organic solidarity
Modern society is not held together by shared beliefs alone but by mutual dependence across specialized roles
keyTakeaways: Sociology's task is to study social facts — phenomena external to and coercive over individuals
The evolution from mechanical to organic solidarity is driven by increases in social density and dynamic density
A healthy modern society requires regular moral regulation of economic forces, not merely laissez-faire
The division of labor produces both cohesion and inequality; justice requires balancing these outcomes
Anomie is both a social pathology and an explanatory concept for modern crises including suicide, economic crisis, and labor conflict
Durkheim's analysis provides an alternative to both conservative nostalgia for tradition and Marxist class-conflict determinism
The pathological coercive division of labor — stratification without meritocracy — is a distinct form of social dysfunction
whoShouldRead: Sociology students seeking to understand the discipline's foundational framework and methodological commitments
Social theorists, political philosophers, and legal scholars investigating the nature of modern social order
Economists and organizational theorists interested in the ethical dimensions of specialization and interdependence
Readers curious about how classical social science diagnoses modernity and its discontents
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for an accessible popular introduction to sociology without sustained theoretical engagement
Those primarily interested in empirical case studies rather than abstract conceptual architecture
Readers who prefer Marxian conflict theory and are uninterested in functionalist analysis
